findings.rationale: Both classes represent the same concept of a repairability assessment for products.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific French 100-point repairability scoring methodology, whereas the upstream RepairabilityInfo is a generic repairability-information container, so ours is the narrower concept rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, but displayScore is specifically for repairability while ratingValue is generic.
findings.qaRationale: Both are decimal rating properties, but displayScore is a domain-specific repairability sub-score on a fixed 0-10 scale whereas schema:ratingValue is a generic rating value, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of certification, whereas hasCertification covers all certifications.
findings.qaRationale: RoHS compliance is one specific certification type, so as a property it is narrower than the general hasCertification property covering all certifications.

findings.rationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of certification, whereas Certification Information is a generic link to any certification.
findings.qaRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific regulatory compliance/certification topic, which is narrower than a generic link to any certification information, so ours is the narrower term.

findings.rationale: Device Category is a general class of electronic devices, while WearableSizeGroupEnumeration is a specific enumeration for wearable product sizes.
findings.qaRationale: WearableSizeGroupEnumeration enumerates clothing/wearable size groups and has no conceptual overlap with a category of electronic devices, so it is not the same concept and the BROAD grade is wrong.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the WEEE classification of electronic devices.
findings.qaRationale: Our DeviceCategory spans both ESPR delegated-act categories and WEEE classifications, whereas the upstream weeeCategory is confined to WEEE classification only, making ours the broader concept.

findings.rationale: Device Category is a specific regulatory classification of electronic devices, whereas Additional Product Classification Details is a general product classification beyond GPC; the upstream term is more general.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:AdditionalProductClassificationDetails is a generic wrapper class for any non-GPC classification detail, not a device taxonomy, so our regulatory WEEE/ESPR DeviceCategory is a different concept rather than a true subclass of it.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers generally to IEC 62474, whereas the OUR term specifies a particular version of the Declarable Substance List.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate is a bare vocabulary/standard reference (IEC 62474 itself), not a defined property denoting a version string of the Declarable Substance List, so there is no genuine property-to-property concept match.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a time span in years using QuantitativeValue, but one refers to feature update support while the other refers to warranty duration.
findings.qaRationale: Warranty duration is a legal repair/replacement promise, a fundamentally different concept from the software feature-update support lifetime our term measures, despite both being year-valued durations.

findings.rationale: Refresh Rate is a specific measurement value for display refresh, whereas Measurement Value is a generic numeric value with any unit.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:value is the scalar magnitude slot inside a QuantitativeValue structure, whereas euelec:refreshRate is a named display characteristic whose range IS a QuantitativeValue, so they occupy different roles and are not a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: Both provide explanatory text about a criterion’s scoring or weighting, but the upstream term focuses on weighting usage rather than the score itself.
findings.qaRationale: Our property holds free-text explanation of a repair criterion's score, whereas the upstream term explains how a criterion's weighting is to be applied—a distinct aspect (weighting methodology vs. score justification), so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both provide explanatory text for a rating/criterion score, but the schema property is intended for short explanations while ours allows detailed descriptions.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying free-text explanation of a score, but schema:ratingExplanation is bound to a Rating (and fact-check context) while ours explains a repair-criterion score, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The WEEE registration number is a specific type of register entry, whereas registryEntry covers any kind of register link.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern an entry in an official register, but ours is a literal string identifier (the producer's WEEE number) while gs1:registryEntry models a link to a register-entry resource, so the semantics overlap rather than cleanly subsume.

findings.rationale: WEEE registration is a specific EPR scheme, whereas eprRegistrationNumber refers to producer registration for any EPR program.
findings.qaRationale: WEEE registration is a specific instance of EPR producer registration, so our term is a narrower specialization of the general eprRegistrationNumber property.

findings.rationale: membershipNumber is a generic unique identifier for any membership, whereas weeeRegistrationNumber refers specifically to the producer’s number in the WEEE register.
findings.qaRationale: A statutory WEEE producer-register registration number is a regulatory compliance identifier, not a membership identifier of a generic membership, so the concepts do not match.

findings.rationale: repairabilityIndex is a numeric score, whereas repairabilityClass denotes a categorical classification; the index is a more specific measurement within that broader class.
findings.qaRationale: Both are repairability-assessment properties but a numeric national index and a categorical class are parallel facets rather than a clean subsumption, so they overlap without one being strictly narrower than the other.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the same WEEE Directive equipment categories defined in the EU regulation.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes plainly keyed to WEEE Directive equipment categories, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm identical scope (6-category enumeration vs. a general category concept), so identity cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is preferred.

findings.rationale: WEEE Category is a specific set of equipment categories, whereas CategoryCode is a generic concept for any category code.
findings.qaRationale: WEEECategory is an enumeration class of six specific WEEE Directive equipment categories, whereas schema:CategoryCode is a generic code-in-a-codelist type; ours is an instance/value set rather than a narrower subtype of the generic code concept, so it is not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The euelec:energyEfficiency property covers the full EU Energy Label information, whereas schema.org’s energyEfficiencyScaleMin specifies only the minimum class on the scale.
findings.qaRationale: Our property carries the full EU Energy Label (the entire scale, the product's class, plus related label data), whereas schema.org's energyEfficiencyScaleMin captures only one datum—the least efficient class on the scale—so ours is broader, not narrower; from our term's perspective the upstream is a single component, making ours BROAD.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named energyEfficiency and intended to capture EU Energy Label information for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Upstream oec:energyEfficiency is the cross-cutting core property with no definition, while ours is the electronics-specific EU Energy Label specialization (range euelec:EnergyEfficiency), so ours is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The schema.org property covers a broader set of energy‑efficiency categories (rating or certification), whereas the euelec property specifically captures EU Energy Label information.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-level energy-efficiency properties that overlap, but neither subsumes the other cleanly: ours is EU-scoped (narrower) yet conveys general 'EU Energy Label information' rather than only a category rating/certification (broader), so it overlaps without identity or clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the energy efficiency category of a product, but schema.org’s hasEnergyConsumptionDetails is broader, covering any international standard rather than specifically the EU Energy Label.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product properties carrying an energy-efficiency rating, but ours is scoped to the EU Energy Label whereas schema.org's covers any international energy-efficiency standard, making ours the narrower term.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the EU energy label classification of a product.
findings.qaRationale: Our energyEfficiency carries the full EU Energy Label information (range EnergyEfficiency object), whereas the upstream energyEfficiencyClass denotes only the single A–G classification value, making ours the broader property.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named repairabilityClass and refer to the same EU Right to Repair classification for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for a product's repairability class, but ours is specifically bound to the EU Right to Repair A-E grading (domain RepairabilityIndex, range EURepairabilityClass) while the undefined core term appears to be a generic cross-cutting version, so they overlap without being provably interchangeable.

findings.rationale: euelec:weeeCompliance provides specific WEEE Directive compliance details, whereas gs1’s compulsoryAdditionalInformation covers all legally required product information.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:compulsoryAdditionalInformation is free-text label/packaging information legally required to be printed, whereas euelec:weeeCompliance is a structured WEEE Directive compliance record (range euelec:WEEECompliance), so they are different in kind rather than broader/narrower instances of one concept.

findings.rationale: WEEE compliance is a specific type of legally required label information, which falls under the broader concept of compulsory additional label information.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a structured WEEE regulatory-compliance object, whereas the GS1 term denotes literal text printed on the product label/packaging; these are different concepts, not a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: euelec:weeeCompliance supplies specific WEEE Directive compliance details, whereas gs1’s compulsoryAdditionalLabelInformationType only indicates the code for any legally required label information type.
findings.qaRationale: Our term carries structured WEEE Directive compliance information about a product, whereas the GS1 term is merely a code denoting which type of compulsory label text appears on packaging, so they are different concepts rather than a genus/species pair.

findings.rationale: WEEE Compliance is a specific type of certification, whereas Has Certification covers all certifications.
findings.qaRationale: Regulatory WEEE-Directive compliance information is not a subtype of third-party certification credentials; compliance status and certification are distinct concepts that only loosely overlap.

findings.rationale: euelec:criterionScore is a specific numeric score for a repair criterion, whereas schema.org’s ratingValue is a general content rating; the former is a narrower use of the latter.
findings.qaRationale: Both express a numeric value on a bounded rating/scoring scale, but schema:ratingValue is the value of a schema:Rating while euelec:criterionScore measures a repair criterion (domain RepairCriterion, not a Rating), so they overlap without a clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: euelec:SoftwareSupport covers all OS/firmware support details, while dppk’s softwareUpdatePolicy is a specific policy about updates; the former is a broader concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes; our SoftwareSupport spans the full OS/firmware support picture (update periods, patches, end-of-support dates) whereas dppk's softwareUpdatePolicy is confined to update policy, a subset of that scope.

findings.rationale: Both properties quantify horizontal pixel count, but one refers to display resolution while the other refers to image file dimensions.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:filePixelWidth describes the pixel dimensions of an image media asset, whereas our term describes a display device's native horizontal resolution; sharing the unit of horizontal pixels is not conceptual overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a version string of software, but euelec:firmwareVersion is specifically for device firmware whereas schema.org’s softwareVersion applies to any software instance.
findings.qaRationale: Firmware version is a specific subtype of the general software version string that schema.org's softwareVersion denotes, so our term is properly narrower (skos:broadMatch target), not merely overlapping.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a version identifier for software, though assemblyVersion is broader and not limited to firmware.
findings.qaRationale: schema:assemblyVersion denotes the version of a software assembly/technology dependency (e.g., .NET Framework 4.5), a distinct concept from embedded device firmware version; the only commonality is the generic notion of a version string, which is name-level not meaning-level overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the date a formal assessment/audit was completed, though one is for repairability and the other for certification.
findings.qaRationale: A repairability-index assessment date and a certification-audit-completion date are distinct concepts tied to different processes, so they are not the same term despite both being evaluation dates.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the date a formal assessment/audit was performed, though one refers to repairability and the other to certification.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org:auditDate is the date a certification was audited (domain Certification), whereas our property dates a repairability index assessment of a product, so despite both being evaluation dates they denote different concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, but schema.org’s ratingValue is generic while our term specifically denotes a repairability score.
findings.qaRationale: schema:ratingValue is a generic subjective rating attached to a Rating node, not a regulated 0-100 sum-of-five-criteria repairability index, so it is a different concept and matching it would misrepresent the regulatory metric.

findings.rationale: euelec:modelIdentifier specifies a textual model number, while schema.org’s model accepts either a URL or text; the upstream term is more general.
findings.qaRationale: schema:model denotes the product's model and may reference a full ProductModel entity or URL as well as a textual model identifier, so our text-only manufacturer model number is a narrower subset of it.

findings.rationale: The modelIdentifier applies to all products, whereas vehicleIdentificationNumber is a specific identifier for motor vehicles.
findings.qaRationale: VIN is a per-instance serial number identifying an individual vehicle, whereas modelIdentifier names a model/SKU class — instance-level vs model-level identity, so not the same concept (no genuine broader/narrower subsumption).

findings.rationale: euelec:modelIdentifier specifies a manufacturer’s model number, whereas schema.org’s identifier is a generic property for any kind of identifier; the upstream term is more general.
findings.qaRationale: A manufacturer's model number is a specific kind of the generic schema.org identifier property, so our term is narrower than the upstream term.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name and intended use for EU energy label classes, indicating identical concepts.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for energy efficiency class, but ours is constrained to the EU Energy Label A-G scale while the cross-cutting core property is undefined and presumably generic, making ours the narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: The upstream property refers to a general energy efficiency category, while the OUR term specifies the EU Energy Label class (A‑G).
findings.qaRationale: Both are energy-efficiency properties, but schema's hasEnergyConsumptionDetails links a product to a structured EnergyConsumptionDetails object whereas ours carries the EU A-G class value directly, so they overlap without a clean subsumption (the true narrow-match target would be schema's EUEnergyEfficiencyEnumeration).

findings.rationale: euelec:energyEfficiencyClass specifies the EU A‑G label, while schema.org’s hasEnergyEfficiencyCategory covers any international rating or certification; the upstream term is more general.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying an energy-efficiency rating, but ours is restricted to the EU A–G Energy Label while schema.org's hasEnergyEfficiencyCategory covers any international standard's rating or certification, so our term is the narrower one (the first pass had the direction reversed).

findings.rationale: euelec:componentPassport refers specifically to a component’s own nested DPP, whereas gs1:Digital Product Passport is a general link to any product passport; the upstream term is more general.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties whose value is a link to a digital product passport; ours restricts that link to the specific case of a component's nested DPP, making it a narrower specialization of the general gs1:dpp link.

findings.rationale: Component Passport is a specific type of traceability link (to a nested DPP), whereas the GS1 Traceability information property is a general link to any traceability data.
findings.qaRationale: A component-passport link points to a sub-component's nested DPP (a compositional/bill-of-materials reference), which is a different concept from GS1 traceability information (track-and-trace history), so it is not a narrower form of the same property.

findings.rationale: euelec:weeeRegistrationCountry specifies a country code for WEEE registration, whereas schema.org’s addressCountry is a general country property; the upstream term is broader.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific WEEE-registration country code constrained to ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 on a WEEECompliance domain, a narrower specialization of schema.org's general addressCountry, so ours is narrower not broader.

findings.rationale: Both properties use ISO 3166‑1 country codes, but the upstream term refers to a generic address country while ours specifies the WEEE registration country.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a property of a WEEE compliance record identifying the jurisdiction where a producer is registered, whereas gs1:addressCountry is a postal-address component property on a different domain, so they are not the same concept despite both encoding ISO 3166-1 codes.

findings.rationale: euelec:weeeRegistrationCountry specifies a country code for WEEE registration, whereas gs1:countryCode is a general ISO 3166‑1 country code for addresses; the upstream term is broader.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:countryCode is definitionally scoped to the country of a postal address, whereas weeeRegistrationCountry denotes a regulatory registration jurisdiction; sharing the ISO 3166-1 code space is datatype overlap, not concept identity, so it is not a true narrowing of the address-country property.

findings.rationale: The upstream property has no definition and its label does not indicate a WEEE registration country.
findings.qaRationale: WEEE registration is a specific EPR scheme, so our WEEE registration country is a narrower instance of the upstream EPR jurisdiction (country where an EPR registration applies).

findings.rationale: The upstream property describes a general price specification, while ours specifies the price of a component used as a spare part.
findings.qaRationale: Both are price-association properties pointing to a PriceSpecification; ours restricts the general schema:priceSpecification to the specific spare-part price, making it a genuine sub-property (narrower).

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the version identifier of a software or firmware instance.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a software/firmware version string, but euelec:newVersion specifically means the post-update version in a before/after update context, whereas schema:softwareVersion is the generic version of any software instance, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a version identifier for software or firmware, but schema.org’s assemblyVersion is more general to product/technology versions.
findings.qaRationale: Our term denotes the resultant version after a firmware/software update event (implying a before/after pair), whereas schema.org assemblyVersion is a static general product/technology version label, so they are not the same concept despite the shared word 'version'.

findings.rationale: Both properties capture a version identifier for an entity, though schema.org’s applies to CreativeWork while ours is generic software/firmware.
findings.qaRationale: Both are version-identifier properties, but ours denotes specifically the post-update software/firmware version (a role within an update event) rather than schema.org's generic CreativeWork version, so they overlap without being interchangeable or a clean subtype.

findings.rationale: Both properties quantify vertical pixel counts, but one applies to display resolution while the other refers to image file dimensions.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:filePixelHeight describes the vertical pixel dimension of an image file (digital asset), whereas our term describes a display device's native vertical screen resolution; despite both counting vertical pixels, they measure different concepts and are not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: euelec:screenResolutionHeight specifies pixel height of a display, which is a more specific instance of the generic item height defined by schema.org/height.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/height is a generic physical/spatial dimension of an item (a length measure), whereas euelec:screenResolutionHeight is a count of pixels along the vertical axis—a different concept (pixel count, not physical height), so it is not a narrowing of the same concept.

findings.rationale: euelec:screenResolutionHeight specifies pixel height of a display, which is a more specific instance of the generic height property in dppk.
findings.qaRationale: A generic untyped dppk 'height' property (no definition, no domain) is a physical/dimensional measure of a product, not the vertical pixel-count of a display's screen resolution, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: euelec:screenDiagonal is a specific measurement of screen size, whereas gs1:Measurement Value is a generic numeric value with unit; the former is a narrower instance of the latter.
findings.qaRationale: euelec:screenDiagonal is a domain-specific attribute whose range is a gs1:QuantitativeValue, whereas gs1:value is the generic float-component held inside such a value object; one references the structure the other is a part of, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: euelec:DisplaySpecification is a specific set of technical specs for screens, whereas schema.org/PropertyValueSpecification is a generic specification of any property value.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/PropertyValueSpecification defines input constraints for a property value in web/form Actions (min/max, required, etc.), which is an entirely different concept from a product's display technical specifications, so it is not a broader version of our term but an unrelated one.

findings.rationale: euelec:DisplaySpecification focuses on technical specs for screens, whereas schema.org/SizeSpecification is a generic size description applicable to many products.
findings.qaRationale: euelec:DisplaySpecification covers display technical specs (resolution, panel type, brightness) while schema.org/SizeSpecification describes physical size/dimensions and apparel sizing, so they are not the same concept and ours is not a subtype of it.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the same concept of annual energy consumption measured in kWh.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for annual energy consumption, but ours is explicitly constrained to kWh via gs1:QuantitativeValue while the upstream oec: term has no stated definition, range, or unit, so identical meaning cannot be confirmed and a weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the fraction of a device that can be recovered (recycling + energy recovery) on a 0–1 scale.
findings.qaRationale: Recoverability (recycling + energy recovery) is a regulatory superset of recyclability (recycling only), so our term is broader, not interchangeable as the first pass claimed.

findings.rationale: euelec:displayTechnology specifies a particular display type, whereas schema.org/imagingTechnique is a broader term covering any imaging method.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/imagingTechnique denotes a medical/diagnostic imaging method (X-ray, MRI, ultrasound), an entirely different concept from a display panel's rendering technology (LCD/OLED), so they are not the same concept in any broader/narrower sense.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a specific repairability criterion, while the upstream class is a generic evaluation condition.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is a domain-specific repairability criterion that is a true specialization of the generic CCCEV evaluation Criterion, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: RepairCriterion represents a single criterion score, whereas RepairabilityInfo is a broader class describing overall repairability information.
findings.qaRationale: A single repairability-index criterion score is a constituent part of, not a narrower kind of, a general repairability-info container, and the upstream class has no definition to confirm even a part-whole alignment, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our class is a specific repairability criterion, whereas the upstream class represents a generic evaluation condition.
findings.qaRationale: A repairability index sub-criterion is a specific species of UNTP's generic evaluation Criterion, so ours is narrower, though the upstream's conformity-assessment framing only partially overlaps our scoring-index sense.

findings.rationale: RepairCriterion is a specific type of numeric score, whereas Rating is a general concept for any numeric evaluation.
findings.qaRationale: A repair criterion is a domain-specific numeric evaluation score, making it a specialized subtype of schema:Rating's general numeric-evaluation concept.

findings.rationale: securitySupportYears is a specific numeric measurement of security update duration, whereas Measurement Value is a general numeric value with unit code.
findings.qaRationale: securitySupportYears is a domain property denoting security-update duration, whereas gs1:value is a generic structural numeric slot inside a QuantitativeValue, so they are not the same concept and our term is not a narrower kind of it.

findings.rationale: Both refer to the origin of an event, but updateSource is specifically for software/firmware updates, making it a narrower specialization of schema.org/source.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/source denotes the source or cause of an Event entity, whereas euelec:updateSource is a string naming the delivery channel of a firmware/software update (OTA, manual, service center); the match is lexical not conceptual.

findings.rationale: euelec:osVersion specifies the version of an operating system, which is a specific type of software instance; schema.org/softwareVersion refers to any software version.
findings.qaRationale: Both are version-string properties, and an operating system is a specific kind of software instance, so euelec:osVersion is a narrower specialization of schema.org/softwareVersion.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the current OS version, while the candidate lists supported operating systems in general.
findings.qaRationale: schema:operatingSystem names which OS platforms are supported (e.g. Windows, Android), whereas euelec:osVersion is the version string of the current OS — these are distinct concepts, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a date marking the end of validity for a specific provision (security updates vs certification), sharing similar scope but differing in context.
findings.qaRationale: Both are end-dates but denote unrelated concepts—cessation of software security update provision versus lapse of a certification's validity—so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a date marking the conclusion of an activity, but euelec:securityUpdateEndDate is specific to security updates while schema.org/endDate is a general end date.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific subtype of a generic temporal end-point, so it is properly narrower than schema.org/endDate rather than overlapping-but-distinct, though the match rests only on the shared abstract notion of an ending date.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a date marking the end of validity for a specific provision (security updates vs regulatory reference applicability), sharing similar scope but differing in context.
findings.qaRationale: Our term marks the end of a vendor's security-update provisioning commitment for software, whereas the GS1 term marks when a cited regulatory reference stops being applicable; these are distinct concepts beyond both merely being end-dates.

findings.rationale: Our class encompasses EU energy label data, metrics and registry information, whereas the candidate only enumerates the efficiency classes.
findings.qaRationale: Schema's term is a value enumeration of efficiency-class labels (A-G, A+++), whereas ours is a data-container class aggregating power-consumption metrics and EPREL registry info; the enumeration is at most the range of one of our properties, not a narrower form of the same concept, so a class-to-class graded match does not hold.

findings.rationale: euelec:EnergyEfficiency focuses on EU Energy Labeling Regulation data, whereas schema.org/EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration covers a broader set of international energy efficiency standards.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration is a controlled enumeration of rating values (A-G classes), whereas euelec:EnergyEfficiency is a composite data class aggregating efficiency class plus power-consumption metrics and EPREL registry info, so they are different kinds of concept rather than broader/narrower forms of the same one.

findings.rationale: Our class focuses specifically on EU Energy Label data and EPREL registry information, whereas the candidate covers all energy consumption details for products under various international regulations.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes modeling product energy-efficiency/labeling information, but ours is constrained to the EU Energy Label scheme (A-G classes, EPREL registry, Reg. 2019/2021) while schema.org's class spans international regimes including the US EPCA rule, making ours a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same IRI and label, indicating they represent the EPREL registration number for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties naming a product's EPREL registration number identifier, semantically interchangeable despite the upstream's missing definition.

findings.rationale: The EPREL registration number is a specific identifier within the EU Energy Label registry, which falls under the broader concept of a regulatory verification number.
findings.qaRationale: EPREL registration number identifies a product's own registration record in the EU energy-label registry, whereas a GS1 regulatoryVerificationNumber is a verification/access credential used alongside a reference number to retrieve a stored document, so they are different concepts and the property is also not a generic verification number.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the EPREL registration number, a particular type of regulatory reference number; the candidate is a generic regulatory reference number.
findings.qaRationale: EPREL is a regulatory product registry, so our EPREL registration number is a specific instance of the candidate's generic regulatory reference number, making ours narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream property lacks a definition, so we cannot confirm it matches the defined Power Consumption Off concept.
findings.qaRationale: The identical local name and matching property type in our own core layer make this almost certainly the same 'power consumption when off but plugged in' concept, but the absent upstream definition prevents confirming full interchangeability, so CLOSE is the safest non-rejecting grade.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the power drawn by a device while it is switched off but still plugged in, i.e., standby consumption.
findings.qaRationale: Off-mode power (switched off but plugged in) and standby-mode power are distinct, separately-regulated power states under EU Ecodesign, so the two properties measure different concepts despite both being low-power consumption metrics.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the proportion of a device that can be recycled, expressed as a fraction between 0 and 1.
findings.qaRationale: Our term scopes recyclability to an electronics device via the WEEECompliance domain, making it a narrower specialization of the generic core recyclabilityRate property (whose missing definition rules out asserting EXACT).

findings.rationale: Both refer to an end date, but the schema.org property is a general item end date whereas our term specifically denotes the end of feature/OS updates.
findings.qaRationale: schema:endDate is a generic temporal endpoint for an item itself, carrying no software-support semantics and not denoting the end of one sub-aspect like feature updates, so ours is not a true narrowing of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties are URLs that provide information about a specific scheme or program, though the subject matter differs.
findings.qaRationale: Both are merely URL-typed properties but address entirely unrelated domains (WEEE e-waste take-back schemes vs. health insurance plan brochures), so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL that directs to information about compliance with WEEE/EPR collection or take‑back schemes, though the candidate lacks a detailed definition.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific WEEE collection/take-back scheme URL whereas the candidate is a generic EPR-compliance URL property, and absent any candidate definition I cannot confirm they denote the same concept rather than merely overlapping naming.

findings.rationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of regulatory information, whereas Regulatory Information covers all government regulations for a trade item.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes about product regulatory data, and RoHS compliance is a specific instance of the broader 'applicable government regulations' concept, so our term is genuinely narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream class is a generic regulatory notification, whereas our term specifically denotes RoHS Directive compliance.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a compliance-status record about a product's RoHS conformity, whereas the GS1 class is a notification message conveying regulatory information, so they are different concepts (a record vs. a message) and not merely broader/narrower.

findings.rationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of certification, whereas Certification covers all kinds of official attestations.
findings.qaRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific regulatory compliance/attestation that fits as a narrower kind of schema.org Certification, though the match is approximate since compliance status differs subtly from a formal third-party certification.

findings.rationale: availableChannel is a general means of accessing a service, while updateChannel specifically denotes the URL or method for obtaining software updates.
findings.qaRationale: schema:availableChannel is an object property linking a Service to a ServiceChannel describing how to access that service, whereas euelec:updateChannel is a literal URI for software-update delivery; the conceptual purpose and range (ServiceChannel object vs xsd:anyURI) differ enough that this is not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The Update Channel property is a specific type of channel used for software updates, whereas hasChannel refers to any communication channel for public services.
findings.qaRationale: hasChannel links a Public Service to a Channel entity (office, phone, online service) for citizen interaction, whereas updateChannel is a URL/method for delivering software updates in a SoftwareSupport context — the shared word 'channel' is the only overlap, with mismatched domain, range, and concept.

findings.rationale: The property has the same IRI and identical semantics as the upstream candidate.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties denoting the URL to a product's page in the EPREL database; the euelec term is a regulation-scoped alias of the identically-named oec core property and is fully interchangeable in meaning.

findings.rationale: The OUR class covers all WEEE compliance information, while the candidate term represents only a specific category within that compliance.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a compliance-information aggregate (category, registration number, collection scheme), whereas the candidate denotes only the WEEE equipment category itself; a category is a constituent attribute, not a narrower kind of compliance record, so they are different concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: WEEE Compliance is a specific type of regulatory compliance for the WEEE Directive, whereas RegulatoryInformation covers all applicable government regulations.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes and WEEE compliance is a specific instance of regulatory information about a trade item, so ours is properly narrower than the generic gs1:RegulatoryInformation.

findings.rationale: RepairCriterionType specifies individual criteria of the French Repairability Index, whereas RepairabilityInfo is a broader class covering all repair‑related information.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a controlled enumeration of scoring-criterion types within the French Repairability Index, an ontologically different kind of thing from an undefined repairability-information aggregate, so there is no genuine subsumption (NARROW) relation between them.

findings.rationale: Repair Criterion Type is a specific instance of the generic Criterion concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a domain-specific enumeration of the five French Repairability Index criteria, which is a proper specialization of the generic CCCEV Criterion class (both classes), so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Repair Criterion Type is a specific instance of the generic Criterion concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our enumeration of French Repairability Index criterion types overlaps with UNTP's generic conformity Criterion class but sits at a type/category level in a scoring (not conformity-assessment) context, so it is not cleanly a subclass.

findings.rationale: ReplacementDifficulty describes a specific aspect of repairability (difficulty level), while RepairabilityInfo is a broader class encompassing all repair‑related information.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a difficulty-level/measurement concept for component replacement, whereas the undefined upstream RepairabilityInfo is a general repair-information container; they are different kinds of class, not a genuine genus-species (NARROW) relation, so reject.

findings.rationale: The RoHS Declaration URL is a specific type of referenced file, whereas referencedFileURL is a generic website URL.
findings.qaRationale: Both are URL-valued properties and our RoHS declaration URL is a specific specialization of the generic gs1:referencedFileURL (referenced document URL), so ours is genuinely narrower.

findings.rationale: The schema.org enumeration includes all EU classes plus additional A+, A++, and A+++ levels, making it a broader concept than the EU Energy Label classes A‑G.
findings.qaRationale: Both classes denote the EU energy-label efficiency-class concept and overlap substantially, but they reference different regulation versions/value sets (A-G vs A-G plus A+/A++/A+++) and our term is a plain class while the upstream is an enumeration type, so they are not interchangeable; the differing enumeration membership reflects a regulatory version, not a genuine subtype relation, so NARROW is incorrect.

findings.rationale: The EU Energy Label classes (A‑G) are a specific subset of the broader international energy efficiency levels enumerated by schema.org’s EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration.
findings.qaRationale: Schema.org's EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration is the abstract supertype of all energy-efficiency rating scales (its members include the EU classes via EUEnergyEfficiencyEnumeration), so OUR EU-specific A–G class concept is narrower than upstream, meaning from OUR perspective the relation is the inverse: ours is NARROW relative to upstream — but as a graded match ours sits below it, so the correct directional grade is NARROW, not BROAD.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the EU Energy Label efficiency classes A‑G, sharing identical scope.
findings.qaRationale: The oec: core term is the generic cross-cutting energy-efficiency-class abstraction, while OUR term is scoped specifically to the EU 2019/2021 Energy Label A–G classes, making ours the narrower specialization rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The EU Energy Label classes (A‑G) are a specific subset of the general energy efficiency concept represented by the upstream EnergyEfficiency class.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes in the energy-efficiency domain, and the EU A-G label grades are a specialized subset of the broader, undefined upstream EnergyEfficiency concept, so ours is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The schema.org class represents general energy consumption details, while the OUR term specifies only EU Energy Label classes A‑G.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is one specific facet (the A-G efficiency class label) of schema.org's broader EnergyConsumptionDetails, which encompasses energy-consumption information generally, so ours is the narrower concept.

findings.rationale: The candidate class represents general repairability information, while the OUR term specifies a specific EU Right to Repair grade system.
findings.qaRationale: Both are repairability classes in the same domain, but ours is a specific A-E grading scheme while the undefined upstream appears to be a general repairability information container, so they overlap rather than one strictly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: EU Repairability Class is a specific grading system for repairability, whereas Criterion is a generic condition for evaluation.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a class enumerating repairability grades (A-E), a classification scheme, whereas cv:Criterion models an evaluation condition; these are distinct concepts, not a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same label and refer to standby power consumption, indicating identical concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties naming standby-mode power consumption with identical label and concept, the upstream oec: core term being the cross-cutting anchor for this euelec: specialization, so they are interchangeable despite the upstream lacking an explicit definition.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe power usage when the device is not actively operating; the candidate lacks a specific definition but refers to an equivalent standby/off state.
findings.qaRationale: Standby mode and off mode are distinct, separately-regulated power states under EU ecodesign rules, so a standby-power property is not the same concept as an off-mode power property.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the period during which a component can be obtained as a spare part, though the candidate lacks an explicit definition.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a quantitative duration in years (range QuantitativeValue), whereas the undefined candidate's name 'sparePartsAvailable' reads as an availability flag/boolean rather than a time span, so they measure different things.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the manufacturer's part number for a component or product.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a manufacturer's part number, but ours is scoped to a component (a part within a product) while schema:mpn identifies the product/offer itself, so the referents differ enough to be overlapping rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The upstream property is a generic URL, while our term specifically denotes the EU Energy Label image URL.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific typed property (range xsd:anyURI) for the EU Energy Label image on an EnergyEfficiency class, whereas the upstream is an undefined generic 'url' property with no definition or domain, so they are not the same concept and a graded match would conflate a regulatory artifact link with an arbitrary URL.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the power consumption of a device while it is on/active, using the same quantitative value type.
findings.qaRationale: Identical label and matching property type strongly suggest the same concept, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm full interchangeability (e.g. same unit/measurement basis), so a weaker grade than EXACT is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the highest possible value in a rating or scoring system.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote the ceiling of a scoring scale, but bestRating is scoped to schema:Rating instances while criterionMaxScore characterizes a RepairCriterion's fixed scale, so they overlap strongly without being strictly interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The schema.org property is a general upper bound for any characteristic, while our term specifically denotes the maximum score of a repair criterion.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specialized maximum bounded to a repair criterion's score, whereas schema:maxValue is the generic upper value of any characteristic, so ours is strictly narrower than the upstream property.

findings.rationale: The candidate property points to a product’s SmartLabel page, which is a broader concept than the specific image URL of a repairability label.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 smartLabel links to a general product information page (a class-level product link), whereas our property is the URL of a specific repairability-label image scoped to a RepairabilityIndex, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The schema.org property is a general download link for any binary, whereas our term specifically points to an image of a repairability label.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org downloadUrl is a media object's own binary-download endpoint, whereas our property links a repairability index to an external label image reference, so they are not the same concept despite both being URL-valued.
